metadata.description: Relationship between economic systems, inequality, and tipping practices
metadata.notes: V2 uses length-matched distractors (ratio 0.8-1.2) and balanced certainty levels. All truths and shortest distractors within target ratio.

facts.truth: Higher minimum wages correlate with lower tipping norms.
distractors: Higher minimum wages require explicit legal tipping compliance requirements.
Minimum wage levels show no correlation with tipping practices.
Tipping culture strengthens when minimum wages increase substantially.
facts.context: Comparative study of tipping norms across OECD economies.

facts.truth: Tipping lets employers externalize wage costs onto customer discretion.
distractors: Employers actively discourage tipping to maintain wage predictability throughout.
Tipping systems require employers to pay the full minimum wages.
Tipping creates balanced cost-sharing between employers and customers overall.
facts.context: Labor economics perspective on cost allocation mechanisms.

facts.truth: Tipping reinforces class hierarchies through asymmetric power relationships.
distractors: Tipping systems reduce income inequality by redistributing to workers.
Class distinctions disappear when customers participate in tipping.
Tipping creates egalitarian relationships between all individual participants.
facts.context: Bourdieu's theory of social capital and tipping as class reproduction.

facts.truth: Tip-dependent workers experience higher income volatility and unpredictability.
distractors: Income volatility is lower in tipping systems than other sectors.
Tipping guarantees stable and predictable income for workers.
Tipping eliminates all economic uncertainty in hospitality work.
facts.context: Empirical studies of income stability in tipping vs. salaried service work.

facts.truth: Social pressure in face-to-face transactions increases tips beyond rational levels.
distractors: Customers tip less when service providers observe the transaction directly.
Tipping amounts reflect pure service quality assessment and cost-benefit.
Social expectations have virtually negligible effects on tipping behavior overall.
facts.context: Behavioral economics and public good provision theory.

facts.truth: Female servers face higher baseline expectations based on attractiveness judgments.
distractors: Attractiveness has no measurable impact at all on customer tip decisions.
Tipping rates are identical for male and female service workers.
Male servers consistently receive systematically higher tip amounts overall.
facts.context: Empirical research on gender wage gaps in tipped occupations.

facts.truth: Menu prices are lower in high-tipping regions because total cost shifts to tips.
distractors: Restaurants maintain identical menu prices in all regions regardless of tipping cultural norms.
Menu prices systematically increase in regions where tipping is culturally very prevalent.
Price-setting decisions in restaurants remain completely independent of all regional tipping norms.
facts.context: Pricing strategy and behavioral anchoring in service industries.

facts.truth: Post-WWII tipping expansion reflects employer cost-externalization strategy.
distractors: Tipping expanded due to increased customer wealth and spontaneous generosity.
Tipping traditions predate any modern wage regulation frameworks.
Wage regulation periods eliminated tipping through legal prohibition.
facts.context: Historical analysis of tipping norms relative to labor law changes.

facts.truth: Occupational racial segregation correlates with tip income gaps in service sectors.
distractors: All racial discrimination in tipping has been entirely eliminated in modern hospitality services.
Tipping amounts are uniformly distributed across all racial groups in service work.
Service workers of all races receive identical average tip incomes consistently.
facts.context: Empirical studies of racial disparities in tipped service sector earnings.

facts.truth: Tipping service sectors concentrate poverty wages among non-degree holders.
distractors: Tipping eliminates the educational wage premium effects entirely and permanently.
Tipping creates well-compensated career paths for workers without degrees.
Service sector wages distribute equally regardless of educational background.
facts.context: Labor market stratification and intergenerational mobility.

facts.truth: Customer guilt responses to visible workers increase tips beyond rational levels.
distractors: Guilt reduces tip amounts as customers resist social pressure dynamics.
Tipping amounts reflect only objective service quality metrics without any emotional factors.
Emotional responses to service workers have zero impact on tips.
facts.context: Psychology of prosocial behavior and social obligation.

facts.truth: Strong social safety nets correlate with lower tipping because work is unionized.
distractors: Strong social safety net strength demonstrates no significant or meaningful relationship to tipping culture whatsoever.
Strong social safety nets actually increase tipping to compensate worker hardship.
Unionized service work actually increases overall tip dependence and tipping percentages substantially.
facts.context: Comparative welfare state analysis and labor market outcomes.

facts.truth: Automated payment systems reduce tipping compared to face-to-face collection.
distractors: Customers actually tip more when social pressure is removed via anonymity.
Digital payment systems increase tipping amounts due to convenience features.
Automated and face-to-face payment systems produce identical tip rates.
facts.context: Technology adoption and prosocial behavior modification.

facts.truth: Tip-dependent structures reduce bargaining power by fragmenting accountability.
distractors: Tipping workers have stronger collective wage negotiation leverage.
Tipping increases worker bargaining power by creating individual revenues.
Tip income creates transparent wage structures for labor disputes.
facts.context: Labor process theory and control mechanisms in service work.

facts.truth: Age bias in tipping creates lower expectations for elderly service workers.
distractors: Worker age provides no variation at all in tip income across service sectors.
Older servers receive systematically higher tips due to their perceived wisdom and experience.
Customer tipping decisions are completely and entirely unaffected by worker age or seniority.
facts.context: Age discrimination in paid and unpaid service work.

facts.truth: Medieval systems expected gratuities to supplement employer-provided servant wages.
distractors: Feudal tipping systems explicitly guaranteed minimum supplemental compensation to servants.
Tipping originated in modern market economies as purely voluntary rewards.
Medieval systems actively prohibited gratuities to maintain wage clarity.
facts.context: Medieval and early modern economic history of service compensation.

facts.truth: Tip percentages signal status and cultural sophistication rather than service quality.
distractors: Status signaling actually reduces rather than increases overall tip amounts and percentages consistently.
Tipping percentages reflect only pure and objective service quality evaluation metrics and assessment.
Customers completely ignore all social norms and cultural expectations when determining tip amounts.
facts.context: Veblen's conspicuous consumption theory applied to tipping.

facts.truth: Migrant workers face language barriers that reduce tip income relative to native workers.
distractors: Cultural differences enhance overall tipping rates significantly in diverse service settings overall.
Language barriers increase tips as customers actively reward worker service effort consistently.
Migrant and native-born service workers earn consistently identical and equal average tips throughout all regions nationwide.
facts.context: Immigration effects on wage stratification in low-wage service work.

facts.truth: Restaurants pass business risk to workers, concentrating volatility among poor workers.
distractors: Employer-absorbed financial risk increases significantly when tipping becomes the primary compensation mechanism.
Tip-dependent service workers experience significantly less income volatility than salaried employees overall.
Tipping systems distribute business risks equally between workers and employers.
facts.context: Precarity theory and risk distribution in capitalist labor markets.

facts.truth: Tipping scripts anchor behavior, reducing calculation and increasing total transfers.
distractors: Tipping scripts completely eliminate all social influence on payment decisions.
Customers independently calculate tips without any reference to social norms.
Suggested tip percentages actually decrease customer monetary contributions below their natural levels.
facts.context: Behavioral anchoring and nudge theory in economic transactions.
